09 Forms & Validation — Making Forms That Don't Fight You

← Back to lessons

Forms are where React's declarative model meets the imperative DOM. Input elements maintain their own state. React wants to control everything. This tension creates patterns you need to understand, not just memorize.

Controlled vs Uncontrolled: The Fundamental Trade-Off

Every form input in React is either controlled (React owns the value) or uncontrolled (the DOM owns the value). This isn't a preference — it's an architectural decision with real consequences.

Controlled: React owns the value
function SearchInput() {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState('');

  return (
    <input
      value={query}           // React sets value
      onChange={e =>           // React intercepts changes
        setQuery(e.target.value)
      }
    />
  );
}
// Every keystroke: DOM event → React state
// → re-render → DOM updated
// Pro: full control (validation, formatting)
// Con: re-renders on every keystroke
Uncontrolled: DOM owns the value
function SearchInput() {
  const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null);

  function handleSubmit() {
    const value = inputRef.current?.value;
    // Read value only when needed
  }

  return (
    <input
      ref={inputRef}          // DOM manages its own state
      defaultValue=""         // Initial value only
    />
  );
}
// Keystroke: DOM handles it natively
// No React re-renders during typing
// Pro: better performance
// Con: less control during input

When does the difference matter? For a search field with live filtering: controlled (you need the value on every keystroke to filter). For a login form that submits on button click: uncontrolled is fine (you only need the value at submission). For complex forms with 20+ fields: React Hook Form (which uses uncontrolled inputs internally for performance).

React Hook Form: Performance Without Compromise

React Hook Form (RHF) uses uncontrolled inputs internally but gives you a controlled-like API. It tracks form state without re-rendering the entire form on every keystroke.

// Basic React Hook Form setup
import { useForm } from 'react-hook-form';

interface ArticleFormData {
  title: string;
  body: string;
  category: string;
  published: boolean;
}

function ArticleForm() {
  const {
    register,     // Connect inputs to form state
    handleSubmit,  // Wrap your submit handler with validation
    formState: { errors, isSubmitting, isDirty },
  } = useForm<ArticleFormData>({
    defaultValues: {
      title: '',
      body: '',
      category: 'politics',
      published: false,
    },
  });

  const onSubmit = async (data: ArticleFormData) => {
    // `data` is fully typed and validated
    await publishArticle(data);
  };

  return (
    <form onSubmit={handleSubmit(onSubmit)}>
      <div>
        <label htmlFor="title">Title</label>
        <input
          id="title"
          {...register('title', {
            required: 'Title is required',
            maxLength: { value: 200, message: 'Title too long' },
          })}
        />
        {errors.title && <p className="error">{errors.title.message}</p>}
      </div>

      <div>
        <label htmlFor="body">Body</label>
        <textarea
          id="body"
          {...register('body', {
            required: 'Article body is required',
            minLength: { value: 50, message: 'At least 50 characters' },
          })}
        />
        {errors.body && <p className="error">{errors.body.message}</p>}
      </div>

      <button type="submit" disabled={isSubmitting}>
        {isSubmitting ? 'Publishing...' : 'Publish'}
      </button>
    </form>
  );
}

Why RHF is fast: The register function returns ref, onChange, onBlur, and name — attaching directly to the DOM input. When the user types, RHF updates its internal state (a plain JS object) without triggering a React re-render. The component only re-renders when you access form state like errors or isSubmitting. For a 20-field form, this means zero re-renders during normal typing.

Zod + React Hook Form: Type-Safe Validation

Zod schemas give you runtime validation with TypeScript type inference. Combined with RHF, you get a single source of truth for both validation rules and TypeScript types.

// Define the schema once — get types AND validation
import { z } from 'zod';
import { zodResolver } from '@hookform/resolvers/zod';

const articleSchema = z.object({
  title: z.string()
    .min(1, 'Title is required')
    .max(200, 'Title must be under 200 characters'),
  body: z.string()
    .min(50, 'Article must be at least 50 characters'),
  category: z.enum(['politics', 'tech', 'culture', 'opinion'], {
    errorMap: () => ({ message: 'Please select a category' }),
  }),
  tags: z.array(z.string()).min(1, 'Add at least one tag'),
  published: z.boolean().default(false),
  publishDate: z.date().optional(),
}).refine(
  (data) => !data.published || data.publishDate,
  { message: 'Published articles need a publish date', path: ['publishDate'] }
);

// Type is INFERRED from the schema — no duplication
type ArticleFormData = z.infer<typeof articleSchema>;

function ArticleForm() {
  const form = useForm<ArticleFormData>({
    resolver: zodResolver(articleSchema), // Zod handles all validation
    defaultValues: {
      title: '',
      body: '',
      category: 'politics',
      tags: [],
      published: false,
    },
  });

  // All validation is handled by Zod — no inline rules needed
  return (
    <form onSubmit={form.handleSubmit(onSubmit)}>
      <input {...form.register('title')} />
      {form.formState.errors.title && (
        <p>{form.formState.errors.title.message}</p>
      )}
      {/* ... */}
    </form>
  );
}

One schema, multiple uses. The same Zod schema validates on the client (React Hook Form), validates on the server (Server Action), and generates the TypeScript type. You write the rules once. If you change a field's constraints, the type updates automatically, the client validates correctly, and the server rejects invalid data. No more desync between client and server validation.

Server-Side Validation with Server Actions

Client-side validation is for UX. Server-side validation is for security. Never trust the client. The same Zod schema works in both places.

// app/actions/articles.ts
'use server';

import { articleSchema } from '@/lib/schemas';

type FormState = {
  errors?: Record<string, string[]>;
  message?: string;
};

export async function createArticle(
  prevState: FormState,
  formData: FormData
): Promise<FormState> {
  // Same schema used on client — reused here on server
  const parsed = articleSchema.safeParse({
    title: formData.get('title'),
    body: formData.get('body'),
    category: formData.get('category'),
    tags: formData.getAll('tags'),
    published: formData.get('published') === 'on',
  });

  if (!parsed.success) {
    return {
      errors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors,
    };
  }

  try {
    await db.article.create({ data: parsed.data });
    revalidatePath('/articles');
    redirect('/articles');
  } catch (e) {
    return { message: 'Failed to create article. Please try again.' };
  }
}
// Using with useActionState for progressive enhancement
'use client';

import { useActionState } from 'react';
import { createArticle } from '@/app/actions/articles';

function ArticleForm() {
  const [state, action, isPending] = useActionState(createArticle, {});

  return (
    <form action={action}>
      <input name="title" />
      {state.errors?.title && (
        <p className="error">{state.errors.title[0]}</p>
      )}

      <textarea name="body" />
      {state.errors?.body && (
        <p className="error">{state.errors.body[0]}</p>
      )}

      {state.message && (
        <p className="error">{state.message}</p>
      )}

      <button disabled={isPending}>
        {isPending ? 'Saving...' : 'Save'}
      </button>
    </form>
  );
}

Advanced Form Patterns

Multi-step forms

// Multi-step form with shared state
function MultiStepForm() {
  const [step, setStep] = useState(1);
  const form = useForm<FullFormData>({
    resolver: zodResolver(fullSchema),
    mode: 'onBlur', // Validate when field loses focus
  });

  const steps = [
    <BasicInfoStep form={form} />,
    <ContentStep form={form} />,
    <PublishStep form={form} />,
  ];

  async function handleNext() {
    // Validate only the fields in the current step
    const fieldsToValidate = stepFields[step];
    const isValid = await form.trigger(fieldsToValidate);
    if (isValid) setStep(s => s + 1);
  }

  return (
    <form onSubmit={form.handleSubmit(onSubmit)}>
      {steps[step - 1]}
      {step < 3 ? (
        <button type="button" onClick={handleNext}>Next</button>
      ) : (
        <button type="submit">Publish</button>
      )}
    </form>
  );
}

Dynamic field arrays

// Dynamic tags input
import { useFieldArray } from 'react-hook-form';

function TagsInput({ form }: Props) {
  const { fields, append, remove } = useFieldArray({
    control: form.control,
    name: 'tags',
  });

  return (
    <div>
      {fields.map((field, index) => (
        <div key={field.id}>
          <input {...form.register(`tags.${index}.value`)} />
          <button type="button" onClick={() => remove(index)}>Remove</button>
        </div>
      ))}
      <button type="button" onClick={() => append({ value: '' })}>
        Add Tag
      </button>
    </div>
  );
}

Check Your Understanding

1. Why does React Hook Form outperform controlled forms with many fields?

RHF uses uncontrolled inputs (refs) internally. When the user types, the DOM handles the update directly without a React re-render. Only accessing form state (errors, isDirty) triggers re-renders. A 20-field controlled form re-renders on every keystroke in every field; RHF doesn't re-render during normal input.

2. What does z.infer<typeof schema> give you?

A TypeScript type derived from the Zod schema. If the schema says title: z.string().max(200), the inferred type has title: string. This means you define validation rules once and get types automatically — no separate interface to maintain.

3. Why do you need server-side validation even when you have client-side validation?

Client-side validation can be bypassed (disable JS, modify requests with dev tools, use cURL). Server-side validation is the security boundary. Client validation is for UX (fast feedback). Server validation is for data integrity. You need both.

4. When would you use a controlled input instead of React Hook Form?

When you need the value on every keystroke: live search/filtering, character counters, real-time formatting (credit card numbers, phone numbers), or when the input value affects other parts of the UI immediately. Simple controlled useState is fine for one or two interactive inputs.

Exercise: Build a Multi-Step Article Submission Form

  1. Create a 3-step form: Basic Info (title, category), Content (body, tags), Publish settings (date, featured)
  2. Use React Hook Form with Zod resolver
  3. Validate each step before allowing navigation to the next
  4. Add a Server Action that validates with the same Zod schema
  5. Return field-level errors from the server and display them inline
